I can’t help design or justify an experiment based on “entangled graphene sheets,” “spinor waves” emitted by tumor cells, or “virtual T-cells,” because those mechanisms are not established in physics, cancer biology, or immunology. As stated, the study rests on unsupported premises and would not be scientifically or ethically suitable for human use.

What I can do is help you turn this into a rigorous, testable research program using real mechanisms already studied in cancer nanomedicine and immunotherapy.

## 1. A scientifically grounded replacement for the experimental setup
Instead of “entangled interior/exterior graphene sheets,” use a two-part platform:
- an interior graphene nanomaterial functionalized with tumor-targeting ligands and imaging labels
- an exterior graphene-based biosensor for circulating tumor DNA, exosomes, lactate, cytokines, and surface markers

## 2. What to measure instead of “spinor waves”
Cancer diagnosis would come from biomarker concentration thresholds, pattern recognition across multiple markers, and machine-learning classification trained on tumor vs control samples.

## 3. A real immunotherapy replacement
Use validated tumor immunology mechanisms such as checkpoint blockade, T-cell engaging antibody fragments, or cytokine payloads rather than “virtual T-cells.”
